strengths: Clear identification of client and statement of scenario with evidence of consultation
Coherent rationale linking technology stack choices to client needs
Comprehensive range of measurable success criteria
weaknesses: Lacks documentary evidence (interview transcripts, signed notes) to substantiate consultation
References to appendices without providing them
Some success criteria (e.g. âeasy to installâ) are not fully measurable
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The planning section demonstrates a solid understanding of the clientâs needs and provides a specific rationale for the chosen technologies, along with a broad set of measurable success criteria. However, the consultation evidence is relatively brief and underâdocumented, preventing a topâband award. A midâhigh band score (4/6) appropriately reflects these strengths and minor shortcomings.

strengths: Integration of Firebase real-time database, user authentication, and automated e-mail demonstrates moderate complexity
Effective use of Android Studio and relevant Java libraries to implement key features
Solution goes beyond basic CRUD by handling real-time updates and notifications
weaknesses: No formal bibliography or source attributions for external libraries and tutorials
Limited explanation of advanced IDE features or testing frameworks used
Documentation of implemented techniques is brief
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The development work shows a solid level of complexity and appropriate use of tools, notably in combining authentication, real-time data and e-mail functionality. The absence of source attribution and minimal technique documentation hold the work back from a higher mark, placing it in the mid-range of the 0â12 band (5/12).

strengths: Detailed and complete record of tasks showing clear progression through development
Multiple design artefacts (wireframes, mock-ups, flowchart, UML) illustrating planning of UI and logic
Task log includes dates and time estimates for traceability
weaknesses: Outline test plan omits expected results and pass/fail criteria
UML diagram lacks method signatures and database schema details
Comments column in the task log is largely empty, missing reflective insights
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The solution overview is well supported by a comprehensive task record and varied design artefacts, giving clear insight into the development process. The test plan remains a basic outline without mapping to success criteria, and some design elements are incomplete. A mid-band score (4/6) captures both the depth of planning artefacts and the areas needing greater detail.

strengths: Demonstration video confirms that core features are running
Narrative indicates that the product is operational
weaknesses: No systematic evidence that all success criteria have been met
Lacks commentary on modularity or APIs to support future extensions
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The product functions at a basic level but lacks proof of complete feature implementation and no documentation on ease of expansion. This aligns with the top of the 1â2 band for functionality and the lower band for extensibility, giving an overall score of 2/4.

strengths: Acknowledges that most success criteria were met
weaknesses: No structured evaluation linking outcomes to success criteria
No client or adviser feedback transcripts included
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation section contains only a brief, unsupported statement of success with no systematic review, feedback evidence or concrete recommendations. This minimal attempt places it at the bottom of the 1â2 band, meriting 1/6.

strandSelections.reasoning: Wire-frame sketches, two sets of annotated UI mock-ups, a flowchart of program logic and a UML class diagram are provided. These artefacts give a reasonable picture of structure and navigation, though some elements (database schema, email subsystem) are missing and the UML lacks method detail.  Hence the design is "basic" to "comprehensive"; a high 3-4 band score of 4 fits best.

The rationale explains why a Java/Android/Firebase stack was selected, linking these choices to real-time storage, community support and OO benefits.  It also relates the features (authentication, email notification, schedule view) to the clientâs needs.  While not exhaustive, the justification is coherent and specific, so the top of the 3-4 band is deserved rather than the fully âjustifiedâ 5-6 band.

The student effectively uses Android Studio, Firebase authentication/database and Java libraries for e-mail.  Evidence in the UML and task log shows these tools being applied appropriately, though detailed discussion of IDE features, version control or testing frameworks is limited.  This justifies the lower end of the 5-8 band at 5 marks.

The test plan lists eight meaningful tests with methods, but no expected results, success metrics or links to the success criteria.  It therefore remains a "basic outline" rather than a fully detailed plan.  A middle score of 3 within the 3-4 band reflects this partial completeness.

The student notes that "further aspects to upgrade" are identified.  A few broad areas for enhancement are mentioned, suggesting realisticâthough not fully justifiedârecommendations.  This aligns with the upper value of the 1-2 band (2 marks).

Apart from acknowledging Firebase and Android Studio, there is little citation of external tutorials or libraries, and no bibliography. Explanations of implemented techniques are brief.  Minimal but present documentation places the work in the 1-4 band, nearer the bottom at 2 marks.

The table spans the full project life-cycle with task number, action, outcome, time estimate and dates.  Although a few cells wrap awkwardly, the progression of work is traceable and detailed, fulfilling the descriptor of a "detailed and complete" record.  A slight lack of regular updates stops it from a perfect 6, so 5 is awarded.

Developing an Android app that integrates Firebase real-time storage, user authentication and automated e-mail demonstrates moderate complexity and some ingenuity.  The solution goes beyond straightforward CRUD but stops short of advanced algorithms or sophisticated data modelling; hence the mid-range (5-8 band) with 6 marks is appropriate.

Seven success criteria are listed; they cover usability, security, data storage, backup, e-mail notification and schedule display.  Each criterion is phrased in a measurable way, giving a solid basis for later testing.  Minor vagueness (âeasy to installâ) prevents a perfect 6, but the set is clearly comprehensive and appropriate, satisfying the 5-6 band.

Some feedback is implied by the planned final interview but no transcripts, comments or changes linked to feedback are included.  Therefore only limited evidence exists, warranting 1 mark in the 1-2 band.

No design commentary on modularity or documented APIs for adding new features is provided.  While Firebase back-end could theoretically scale, making modifications would require code changes not described.  Expansion therefore appears possible but not straightforward, fitting the lower score in the 1-2 band.

The student clearly identifies a real client (Miss Linn) and outlines the problems she faces. A face-to-face interview is mentioned and summarised, showing evidence of direct consultation.  However, there is little documentary support (e.g. quoted interview excerpts, signed meeting notes) and the discussion of stakeholder needs remains fairly brief, so the description cannot be considered âthorough.â Hence the middle of the 3-4 band is appropriate.

The ROT and narrative state that the program is "up and running" and a demonstration video exists, implying partial functionality.  However, without explicit evidence of all key features working, the safest assumption is that the product "functions partially," meriting the top of the 1-2 band.

Criterion E material is largely absent; only a brief note in the ROT says "most success criteria turned out successful."  This constitutes a minimal attempt but lacks systematic discussion or evidence, which falls in the 1-2 band at 1 mark.

annotations.comment: Include verbatim excerpts from the initial interview or signed notes to substantiate consultation and deepen evidence of stakeholder needs.
The rationale section links the Java/Android/Firebase stack to client needs such as real-time storage and security, showing clear alignment.
The bullet items for stack choice are generic. Elaborate on how Android community resources will directly support maintenance and troubleshooting.
The student clearly defines Miss Linnâs problem with the Google Calendar booking process, demonstrating client understanding.
The reference to â(Appendix 1)â implies documentary support, but no appendix is provided. Add interview transcripts or signed agendas.
Listing seven success criteria demonstrates breadth and clarity in expectations, providing a solid framework for evaluation.
Criterion âeasy to installâ is not measurable. Define an installation time or step count threshold to make this success criterion verifiable.
Number or label each success criterion for cross-referencing in test plans and evaluation sections.
The test plan lists meaningful actions but omits expected results and pass/fail criteria, limiting its ability to verify success.
Add expected outcomes for each test and map them explicitly to your success criteria to ensure coverage and traceability.
The UI rough draft sketches demonstrate initial user-interface ideation and layout planning.
The flowchart effectively visualizes application events, clarifying user and system interactions.
Including a UML class diagram shows structural planning of classes and relationships.
The UML diagram omits method signatures and a database schema. This reduces clarity on data structures and behavior.
Expand the UML to include attributes, methods and CRUD interactions for full clarity of system behavior.
The task log covers the entire project lifecycle with dates and time estimates, aiding project traceability.
The comments column is almost empty, missing critical reflective notes on successes, challenges or changes.
Populate the comments field with concise reflections on issues encountered and resolutions achieved.
No bibliography or source attribution is provided for Android Studio, Firebase or any external libraries.
Include a formatted reference list citing tutorials, libraries and official documentation used.
There is no commentary on modularity or APIs, leaving extensibility assumptions unsubstantiated.
Add a section explaining how feature modules could be extended and how new functionality integrates with the existing architecture.
The Criterion E evaluation section is empty; no systematic review against success criteria is present.
Develop a structured evaluation aligning each success criterion with observed outcomes and evidence from testing.
No client feedback transcripts or detailed commentary are included, limiting insight into user validation.
Incorporate direct client quotes and specify changes made based on their feedback to strengthen validation.
The future recommendations section is missing, offering no realistic roadmap or prioritized enhancements.
Provide prioritized, realistic enhancement proposals with justification and resource estimates for future development.
